items.correct: More distant galaxies recede faster - their recession velocity is proportional to distance.
items.misconception: All galaxies move at the same speed.
items.random_wrong: Galaxies only move in random directions.
items.why: Students miss the proportionality. The expansion isn't motion through space - space itself stretches, so widely separated galaxies appear to move apart faster than nearer ones.

items.q: What does cosmological redshift tell us?
items.correct: Galaxies are receding from us due to the expansion of space.
items.misconception: Galaxies are turning red as they age.
items.random_wrong: Light gets tired and loses energy.
items.why: Students take redshift literally as colour change. It's a WAVELENGTH STRETCH - light waves get longer because space itself expands while light travels, shifting visible light toward the red end of the spectrum.

items.q: Why are Type Ia supernovae used as 'standard candles'?
items.correct: They have nearly uniform peak brightness, so their apparent brightness reveals distance.
items.misconception: Because they happen at regular time intervals.
items.random_wrong: Because they are the brightest objects in the universe.
items.why: Students confuse standard candle with regular timing. The key property is CONSISTENT INTRINSIC BRIGHTNESS - if you know the actual luminosity, the observed dimness tells you the distance.

items.q: On the HR diagram, the y-axis is usually plotted on a:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Log scale. “Linear scale” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A higher B-V index means the star is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cooler and redder. “Hotter and bluer” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many orders of magnitude in luminosity does a full HR diagram typically span?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
